title: Linux Command: chown
summary: A compact learning note covering chown syntax, ownership patterns, recursive mode, output examples, and the most useful chown options for Linux file permission management.
purpose: Use chown to change file and directory ownership safely while confirming results, especially when preparing shared resources or fixing permission issues.
keyPoints: chown changes the owner and optionally the group of files and directories.
Use user:group, user:, or :group syntax to set owner, group, or both.
-R applies ownership changes recursively to directories and nested files.
-v, -c, --from, and --reference are useful for safe, auditable updates.
Use -h or --no-dereference when you need to change symlink ownership itself instead of its target.

notes.title: Basic chown syntax
notes.body: Change the owner of a single file. When only the owner is supplied, the existing group stays the same.

notes.title: Change only the group or both owner and group
notes.body: Use user:group to set both owner and group. Use :group when only the group should change. This is common when files move between user teams or project groups.

notes.title: Recursive ownership changes
notes.body: Use -R to apply ownership changes to a directory and everything inside it. Add -v for feedback on each changed item.

screenshot.title: Recursive chown output
lines: changed ownership of '/var/www/site' from root:root to alice:developers
changed ownership of '/var/www/site/index.html' from root:root to alice:developers

notes.title: Helpful chown options
notes.body: -v shows each file changed, -c reports only when an ownership update actually happens, --from changes ownership only if the file currently has a specific owner:group, and --reference copies ownership from another file.

screenshot.title: Option output examples
lines: changed ownership of 'notes.txt' from bob:staff to alice:developers
changed ownership of 'notes.txt' from bob:staff to alice:developers
changed ownership of 'notes.txt' from bob:staff to alice:developers
changed ownership of 'notes.txt' from bob:staff to alice:developers

notes.title: Symlink ownership and verification
notes.body: Use -h or --no-dereference to change the ownership of a symlink itself. Verify ownership with ls -l or stat after changes.

screenshot.title: Verify ownership output
lines: lrwxrwxrwx 1 bob developers 11 Jun 24 14:02 linkfile -> notes.txt

title: Understanding Linux Permissions
summary: A clear guide to Linux file permissions, permission categories, numeric modes, and special bits like SUID, SGID, and the sticky bit.
purpose: Understand how Linux permissions protect files and directories, how to read permission strings, and why special permissions matter for security and collaboration.
keyPoints: Every file has owner, group, and others permission sets.
Read, write, and execute map to values 4, 2, and 1.
Permissions are shown symbolically (rwx) and numerically (octal).
SUID, SGID, and the sticky bit change runtime behavior and directory deletion rules.
Use ls -l and stat to verify permissions and file type accurately.

notes.title: Permission categories: user, group, others
notes.body: Files and directories use three permission sets: user (u) is the file owner, group (g) is the file's associated group, and others (o) is everyone else. This is the basic access model in Linux.

notes.title: Permission values: read, write, execute
notes.body: Each category has three permissions: read (r=4), write (w=2), and execute (x=1). Add them to get the octal mode for each category, for example 7 (4+2+1) means full permissions.

notes.title: Read symbolic permissions
notes.body: The first character shows the file type: '-' is a regular file, 'd' is a directory, and 'l' is a symlink. The next nine characters are grouped as owner, group, and others permissions.

notes.title: Numeric permission examples
notes.body: Use three digits to set permissions for owner, group, and others. Common modes are 644 for readable files, 755 for executable directories and scripts, and 700 for private files.

notes.title: Special permissions: SUID, SGID, sticky bit
notes.body: Special permissions add extra behavior beyond basic rwx. SUID runs an executable with the owner's privileges, SGID inherits a directory's group for new files, and the sticky bit on directories restricts deletion to file owners.

notes.title: How special permission symbols appear
notes.body: If execute is set and SUID or SGID is present, you'll see 's'. If execute is not set but the special bit is set, you'll see 'S'. The sticky bit appears as 't' when execute is set for others and 'T' when it is not.

title: Using Two GitHub Accounts on One Windows PC with SSH
summary: Configured separate SSH keys and host aliases so one Windows PC can push to both personal and official GitHub repositories without replacing keys.
purpose: Keep personal and official GitHub access separated on the same Windows machine by using different SSH keys and Git remote aliases.
keyPoints: Each GitHub account should have its own SSH key
The SSH config file maps friendly host aliases to different keys
Git remotes use the SSH host alias, not plain github.com
This avoids constantly removing or switching SSH keys between accounts

notes.title: Generate separate SSH keys
notes.body: Create one key for the personal account and one key for the official account. Run these commands from PowerShell.

notes.title: Add both public keys to GitHub
notes.body: Copy each .pub file and add it to the correct GitHub account under Settings > SSH and GPG keys.

notes.title: Create or open the SSH config file
notes.body: The SSH config file tells Windows which key to use for each GitHub account.

notes.title: Configure GitHub host aliases
notes.body: Paste this into the config file. The Host values are custom aliases that Git will use later in the remote URL.

notes.title: Set the repository remote URL
notes.body: Use the matching SSH alias in the Git remote URL. For a personal repository, use github.com-personal. For an official repository, use github.com-official.

notes.title: Test the connection and push
notes.body: Test SSH authentication first, then push normally.

title: Linux Command: touch
summary: A quick teaching note on using touch to create empty files and update file timestamps from the Linux shell.
purpose: Use touch when you need a new empty file for practice, scripts, configuration placeholders, or when you need to refresh a file's access and modification time.
keyPoints: touch creates a file if it does not already exist.
If the file already exists, touch updates its access and modification timestamps.
Multiple files can be created or updated with one command.
The -t option lets you set a specific timestamp in [[CC]YY]MMDDhhmm[.ss] format.

notes.title: Create one empty file
notes.body: This is the most common beginner use: create a blank file in the current directory.

image.alt: Linux terminal showing touch notes.txt and ls -l notes.txt output
image.placeholder: Place your real VM screenshot in public/learning/linux-touch-command/note-1.png, then set src to /learning/linux-touch-command/note-1.png.

notes.title: Create multiple files
notes.body: Pass more than one filename to create or update several files in a single command.

notes.title: Create numbered files with brace expansion
notes.body: Brace expansion lets the shell generate a sequence of filenames before touch runs. This command creates nine empty files named file_1.txt through file_9.txt.

notes.title: Create a file inside a directory
notes.body: touch can create a file inside an existing directory, but it will not create the directory for you. Create the directory first if it does not exist.

notes.title: Create a hidden file
notes.body: In Linux, filenames that start with a dot are hidden by default. touch is often used to create hidden config files.

notes.title: Create files with different extensions
notes.body: You can create multiple files with different names and extensions in one command.

notes.title: Update timestamp only if the file exists
notes.body: Use -c when you only want to update the timestamp of an existing file. If the file does not exist, touch will not create it.

screenshot.title: Terminal output
lines: ls: cannot access 'missing-file.txt': No such file or directory

notes.title: Copy timestamp from another file
notes.body: Use -r to make one file use the same timestamp as another file.

notes.title: Update an existing file timestamp
notes.body: When the file already exists, touch leaves the content unchanged and refreshes the timestamp.

screenshot.title: Terminal output
lines: Size: 0          Blocks: 0          IO Block: 4096   regular empty file

notes.title: Set a custom timestamp
notes.body: Use -t when you need a predictable timestamp for testing, sorting, or demonstrations.

screenshot.title: Terminal output
lines: Size: 0          Blocks: 0          IO Block: 4096   regular empty file
